text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic truncal vagotomy and gastrojejunostomy, beginning with the transection of the gastrohepatic ligament to expose the right cruse. The peritoneum on the inferior aspect of the right cruse is transected using a harmonic scalpel, creating a retroesophageal tunnel while protecting the esophagus. The posterior vagus nerve is dissected out and clipped before being transected with the harmonic scalpel. The dissection proceeds anteriorly, transecting the phrenoesophageal ligament and exposing the left cruse and anterior esophagus. The anterior vagus nerve is separated from the esophagus, clipped, and transected. Finally, the hiatus is closed.

The surgeon performs a laparoscopic truncal vagotomy and gastrojejunostomy, beginning with the transection of the gastrohepatic ligament. The right crus is exposed, and the peritoneum on its inferior aspect is transected using a harmonic scalpel. A retroesophageal tunnel is created with gentle dissection, carefully protecting the esophagus. The posterior vagus nerve is identified and dissected out over a length of three to four centimeters.

Clips are placed on either side of the posterior vagus nerve, followed by transection using the harmonic scalpel. The dissection proceeds anteriorly to the esophagus, transecting the phrenoesophageal ligament, and exposing the left crus and the anterior aspect of the esophagus.

The anterior vagus nerve is visible extending from the left aspect of the mediastinum to the central portion over the esophagus. The surgeon gently separates the nerve from the anterior portion of the esophagus, places clips proximally and distally, and uses the harmonic scalpel to transect the nerve between the clips.
The surgeon closes the hiatus, visually approximating the tissue edges to secure the opening.

The surgeon opens the lesser sac to prepare for the gastrojejunostomy, identifying the posterior wall of the stomach and the ligament of Treitz. The small bowel is measured approximately fifty to sixty centimeters from the ligament of Treitz. A stay suture is placed between the loop of jejunum and the posterior wall of the stomach. Enterotomies are created using the harmonic scalpel, and the gastrojejunostomy is performed with a sixty millimeter blue load stapler. The defect is closed in two layers using 2-0 Vicryl sutures.

Following the completion of the vagotomy, the surgeon opens the lesser sac to prepare for the gastrojejunostomy. The posterior wall of the stomach and the ligament of Treitz are identified. The small bowel is measured approximately fifty to sixty centimeters from the ligament of Treitz. A stay suture is placed between the loop of jejunum and the posterior wall of the stomach. Enterotomies are created using the harmonic scalpel, and the gastrojejunostomy is constructed with a sixty millimeter blue load stapler. The resulting defect is closed in two layers using 2-0 Vicryl sutures.
